title: Project: Notes Organizer
summary: Organize a messy folder into notes, homework, code, and backups using core Linux terminal commands.

sketchBlocks.title: Plan the folder cleanup before moving files
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
A good terminal project starts with a quick plan: **look at what you have, create the folders you need, then move files into place**. That keeps your workspace organized and helps you avoid deleting or moving the wrong thing.

In this project, you are acting like a student cleaning up a messy school folder. You will sort files into folders such as `notes`, `homework`, `code`, and `backups`.

A common first step is to inspect the current workspace:

```bash
pwd
ls
```

- `pwd` shows your current folder.
- `ls` lists what is inside it.

If you see loose files like `biology_notes.txt`, `math_hw.txt`, and `app.py`, you can make destination folders first:

```bash
mkdir notes homework code backups
```

This is better than moving files first and guessing where they should go. The main idea is: **inspect, create structure, then organize**.

sketchBlocks.title: Use move and copy commands for safe organization
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Two of the most useful file commands are `mv` and `cp`.

- `mv` moves a file to a new folder or renames it.
- `cp` makes a copy and keeps the original.

Example:

```bash
mv biology_notes.txt notes/
cp biology_notes.txt backups/biology_notes_backup.txt
```

Line by line:

- `mv biology_notes.txt notes/` puts the original file into the `notes` folder.
- `cp biology_notes.txt backups/biology_notes_backup.txt` creates a second copy in `backups` with a clearer name.

This matters in a real cleanup project because sometimes you want the file in its proper folder **and** a backup copy before making more changes. Use `mv` when the file should live in one new place. Use `cp` when you want an extra copy.

sketchBlocks.title: Check your results by viewing file contents
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
After organizing files, you should verify that the right content ended up in the right place. Linux gives you simple viewing commands for that.

```bash
cat notes/history_notes.txt
wc -l notes/history_notes.txt
```

What each line does:

- `cat notes/history_notes.txt` prints the whole file so you can read it.
- `wc -l notes/history_notes.txt` counts the number of lines in the file.

You can also inspect only part of a file with `head` or `tail` when a file is longer.

For example, if a homework file was moved into `homework/`, using `cat` confirms it still contains the expected text. The big idea is: **organizing files is only half the job; checking their contents confirms the cleanup worked correctly**.

quizDraft.title: Milestone 1: Build the school workspace folders
quizDraft.prompt: You are cleaning up a messy school workspace. In the terminal, inspect the current folder, then create four folders at the top level of the workspace: `notes`, `homework`, `code`, and `backups`. Keep the existing loose files where they are for now. This step matters because a clear folder structure gives you safe destinations before you move anything.
quizDraft.hint: Create the destination folders first, all in the current workspace.

help.concept: `mkdir` creates folders in the current directory, and `pwd` or `ls` helps you confirm where you are before changing the workspace.
help.hint_1: Use `ls` to see the starting files, then make four folders named exactly as the prompt says.
help.hint_2: You can create all four folders with one `mkdir` command if you list each folder name after it.

quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.

quizDraft.title: Milestone 2: Sort files and make one backup
quizDraft.prompt: Continue the same Notes Organizer story. Start from a workspace that already has the folders `notes`, `homework`, `code`, and `backups`. Move `biology_notes.txt` and `history_notes.txt` into `notes/`, move `math_hw.txt` into `homework/`, and move `app.py` into `code/`. Then make a backup copy of `history_notes.txt` inside `backups/` named `history_notes_backup.txt`. This step matters because real organization often combines moving files into categories with keeping one safety copy.
quizDraft.hint: Use `mv` for sorting and `cp` for the backup copy.

help.concept: `mv` changes a file's location, while `cp` creates a second copy. In this task, most files should be moved, but one notes file should also be copied into `backups`.
help.hint_1: After the moves, the top level should mainly contain the four folders, not the original loose files.
help.hint_2: Copy the file after it is inside `notes/`, using its new path as the source for the backup.

quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.

quizDraft.title: Milestone 3: Verify the notes and remove an extra file
quizDraft.prompt: Finish the Notes Organizer project from the already sorted workspace. First, use terminal commands to display the contents of `notes/history_notes.txt` and count its lines. Then remove the extra backup file `backups/history_notes_backup.txt`, leaving the organized folders and the original notes file in place. This step matters because a complete cleanup includes checking file contents and safely deleting an unneeded extra copy.
quizDraft.hint: Use one command to show the file and another to count lines before removing the backup.

help.concept: `cat` prints file contents, `wc -l` counts lines, and `rm` removes a file. This task combines inspection with a careful deletion inside the workspace.
help.hint_1: The file you need to inspect is inside `notes/`, and the file you need to delete is inside `backups/`.
help.hint_2: Run the viewing commands on `notes/history_notes.txt`, then remove only `backups/history_notes_backup.txt`, not the original notes file.

quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
